title: Disability insurance reform in Hungary: Fiscal gains at human cost
summary_en: Case study of Hungary's 2011–2012 disability-insurance reform, which sharply tightened eligibility and re-assessed the incumbent disability-pension caseload. The fiscal saving was large, but documented human costs — re-assessed beneficiaries with worse health and lower earnings post-reform — were significant. The paper compiles the evidence and quantifies the trade-off.
policy_relevance: Direct relevance to ongoing Hungarian disability and rehabilitation policy (KSH, Pénzügyminisztérium, NÉP). The central message: tightening eligibility delivers fiscal gains but transfers costs to a vulnerable group; pairing with rehabilitation services materially reduces the human cost. Useful for parliamentary testimony and EU-level disability policy reviews. Caveat: data limitations on long-run health outcomes.
